operating-system.question: An operating system primarily acts as:
options: an interface between the user and the computer hardware
a database for storing files permanently
operating-system.explanation: The OS manages hardware resources and provides the environment through which users and applications interact with the machine.

operating-system.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of an operating system?
options: Translating C++ source code into machine code
operating-system.explanation: Translation of source code is the job of a compiler, which is an application, not an OS service.

operating-system.question: Loading the operating system into main memory when the computer is switched on is called:
operating-system.explanation: Booting is the startup process in which firmware loads the OS from disk into memory and hands over control to it.

operating-system.question: In a multiprogramming system:
options: only one program resides in memory at a time
several programs reside in memory and share the CPU
programs can run without any memory
the CPU remains idle most of the time by design
operating-system.explanation: Multiprogramming keeps several jobs in memory so that when one waits for I/O, the CPU can switch to another, improving utilisation.

operating-system.question: A time-sharing operating system:
options: gives each active user/process a small time slice of the CPU in turn
runs one job to completion before starting the next
operating-system.explanation: Time sharing rapidly switches the CPU among processes in fixed time slices, so each interactive user gets quick responses.

operating-system.question: Which of the following is an open-source operating system?
operating-system.explanation: The Linux kernel is distributed under an open-source licence; its source code can be freely studied and modified.

operating-system.question: Spooling is most commonly associated with:
options: managing print jobs by buffering them on disk
operating-system.explanation: Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line queues output (typically print jobs) on disk so the slow device can process them while the CPU continues other work.

operating-system.question: The GUI elements Windows, Icons, Menus and Pointer are collectively abbreviated as:
operating-system.explanation: WIMP (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointer) describes the standard components of a graphical user interface.

operating-system.question: A hierarchical file system organises directories in the form of a:
operating-system.explanation: Directories contain subdirectories and files, branching out from a root directory — a tree structure.

operating-system.question: Virtual memory allows a system to:
options: run programs larger than the available physical RAM
run without any RAM at all
permanently increase the size of the hard disk
operating-system.explanation: Virtual memory uses disk space as an extension of RAM, swapping pages in and out so processes can be bigger than physical memory.

data-structures.question: A stack is a data structure that follows the principle:
data-structures.explanation: Insertions (push) and deletions (pop) both happen at the same end — the top — so the last element added is the first removed.

data-structures.question: A queue is a data structure that follows the principle:
data-structures.explanation: Elements are inserted at the rear and removed from the front, so the earliest arrival is served first — First In, First Out.

data-structures.question: Attempting to pop an element from an empty stack causes the condition known as:
data-structures.explanation: Underflow occurs on deletion from an empty structure; overflow occurs on insertion into a full one.

data-structures.question: Binary search can be applied to an array only if the array is:
data-structures.explanation: Binary search repeatedly halves the search interval by comparing with the middle element, which only works when elements are in sorted order.

data-structures.question: Each node of a singly linked list contains:
options: data and the address of the next node
data and the addresses of both neighbouring nodes
only the address of the next node
data-structures.explanation: A singly linked list node stores its data field plus one link (pointer) to the next node; a doubly linked list has links in both directions.

data-structures.question: In a binary tree, each node can have at most:
data-structures.explanation: "Binary" means each node has at most two children, conventionally called the left and right child.

data-structures.question: Which of the following applications typically uses a stack?
options: CPU scheduling of processes in arrival order
data-structures.explanation: Function calls are managed on a call stack: the most recently called function returns first, matching LIFO behaviour.

data-structures.question: Accessing an element of an array by its index takes:
options: time proportional to the array size
time proportional to the index value
longer than searching a linked list
data-structures.explanation: Array elements are stored contiguously, so the address of any element can be computed directly from the index — a constant-time operation.

data-structures.question: Bubble sort works by:
options: repeatedly comparing and swapping adjacent elements
dividing the array into two halves recursively
hashing each element into a table
data-structures.explanation: In each pass, adjacent pairs are compared and swapped if out of order, so the largest remaining element "bubbles" to its correct position.

data-structures.question: Which data structure is most suitable for evaluating or converting arithmetic expressions (e.g. infix to postfix)?
data-structures.explanation: Operators and operands are pushed and popped according to precedence rules, which maps naturally onto stack operations.

cpp-programming.question: Which symbol is used to insert data into cout in C++?
cpp-programming.explanation: The insertion operator << sends data to the output stream, e.g. cout << "Hello"; extraction >> is used with cin.

cpp-programming.question: A function that has the same name as its class and no return type is called a:
cpp-programming.explanation: A constructor automatically initialises objects of the class when they are created; it shares the class name and specifies no return type.

cpp-programming.question: Which OOP feature allows a derived class to acquire properties of its base class?
cpp-programming.explanation: Inheritance lets a new class reuse and extend the data members and functions of an existing class.

cpp-programming.question: Having multiple functions with the same name but different parameter lists is called:
cpp-programming.explanation: Overloading lets several functions share a name as long as their parameter types/counts differ, resolved at compile time.

cpp-programming.question: A destructor in C++ is called:
options: automatically when an object goes out of scope or is deleted
cpp-programming.explanation: The destructor (~ClassName) runs automatically to release resources when an object's lifetime ends.

cpp-programming.question: In C++, data members and member functions of a class are, by default, in scope:
cpp-programming.explanation: Unless explicitly declared otherwise, class members default to private access, hidden from outside the class.

cpp-programming.question: A pure virtual function is declared by:
cpp-programming.explanation: virtual void f() = 0; makes f pure virtual, forcing derived classes to provide their own implementation and making the class abstract.

cpp-programming.question: The keyword used to define a user-controlled access specifier that allows a non-member function to access private data is:
cpp-programming.explanation: Declaring a function as friend grants it access to the private and protected members of the class, despite not being a member itself.

cpp-programming.question: Which of the following best describes function recursion?
options: a function calling another unrelated function
a function calling itself, directly or indirectly
two functions with the same name
a function with no return statement
cpp-programming.explanation: Recursive functions call themselves to solve smaller instances of a problem, terminating via a base case.

cpp-programming.question: In C++, the scope resolution operator :: is used to:
options: define a function outside its class or access a global variable
cpp-programming.explanation: ClassName::function() defines a member function outside the class body; ::variable accesses a global variable hidden by a local one.

html-web-technology.question: HTML stands for:
html-web-technology.explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard markup language for creating web pages.

html-web-technology.question: Which tag is used to create a hyperlink in HTML?
html-web-technology.explanation: The anchor tag text creates a clickable hyperlink to another page or resource.

html-web-technology.question: Which HTML tag is used to define an unordered (bulleted) list?
html-web-technology.explanation: <ul> creates an unordered list; each item within it is marked with <li>. <ol> creates a numbered (ordered) list instead.

html-web-technology.question: Which attribute of the tag specifies the image source file?
html-web-technology.explanation: — src points to the image file location; alt provides fallback text.

html-web-technology.question: CSS is primarily used to:
options: control the structure of web content
control the presentation and styling of web content
html-web-technology.explanation: Cascading Style Sheets separate visual styling (colours, layout, fonts) from HTML's structural markup.

html-web-technology.question: Which HTML tag is used to create a table row?
html-web-technology.explanation: <tr> defines a table row; <td> defines a data cell within it, and <th> a header cell.

html-web-technology.question: Which HTML element is used to embed JavaScript code within a web page?
html-web-technology.explanation: <script> tags embed or link JavaScript, which adds interactivity and dynamic behaviour to otherwise static HTML.

html-web-technology.question: Which HTML tag creates a form for user input?
html-web-technology.explanation: <form> is the container for interactive input controls like <input>, <select> and <textarea>, and defines how data is submitted.

html-web-technology.question: The correct HTML element for the largest heading is:
html-web-technology.explanation: Headings run from <h1> (largest/most important) down to <h6> (smallest); <head> is unrelated, holding document metadata.

html-web-technology.question: The World Wide Web operates mainly using the protocol:
html-web-technology.explanation: HTTP (and its secure form HTTPS) governs how web browsers and servers exchange requests and responses for web pages.
